1: 'Moses went and spoke these words to all Israel' — the chapter transition marks the formal beginning of Moses's farewell address, the final public speech of his life. The phrase 'all Israel' (kol-yiśrāʾēl) signals a formal assembly setting — the same audience formula used for the covenant renewal ceremony of chapter 29 (Deuteronomy 31:2). Moses now speaks not as lawgiver but as the covenant mediator completing his term of office. The 'speaking' (waydabbēr) that has governed all of Deuteronomy since 1:1 is here contextualized as farewell: everything previously spoken is now the legacy being formally handed off.
2: 'I am a hundred and twenty years old today. I am no longer able to lead you out and bring you in' — Moses's age (120 years) divides neatly into three equal periods of forty years: forty in Egypt (Acts 7:23: 'when he was forty years old'), forty in Midian (Exodus 7:7: when he appears before Pharaoh he is eighty), and forty in the wilderness (Deuteronomy 8:2). The literary shaping of his life around three forty-year periods reflects the same wilderness-generation number — the covenant generation's life and Moses's life are co-extensive. 'I am no longer able to lead you out and bring you in' is an administrative formula for military leadership — the same idiom used for Joshua taking command (Joshua 14:11) and for the role of a king (1 Samuel 18:16).
3: 'The LORD your God himself will cross over before you' — the divine advance-crossing is the theological claim that resolves the problem of Moses's incapacitation: it does not matter that Moses cannot cross, because YHWH himself leads the invasion. 'Cross over before you' (ʿōbēr lĕpānêkā) recalls the pillar of cloud leading the Exodus (Exodus 13:21) and YHWH's angel going before Israel (Exodus 23:20,23; Deuteronomy 33:2). Joshua is named as the human executor, but YHWH is the primary agent: 'The LORD has said' — Joshua's leadership is authorized by, and secondary to, the divine mandate.
4: 'The LORD will deal with them just as he dealt with Sihon and Og' — the Sihon-and-Og citation (cf. Deuteronomy 2:24-3:11) functions as the paradigmatic precedent for Cisjordanian conquest. Throughout the conquest narrative (Josh 2-12), Rahab's confession shows that Canaanite awareness of the Sihon-Og precedent has already broken their military morale: 'We heard how the LORD dried up the water of the Red Sea ... and what he did to Sihon and Og' (Joshua 2:10). The two Transjordanian kings serve as the proof-of-concept for every subsequent battle — if YHWH gave them, he will give the others.
5: 'The LORD will hand them over to you, and you must deal with them according to all the commands I have given you' — the handing-over (nātan, give) and the commanded action creates a two-part structure that runs through all conquest narratives: YHWH's gift of the enemy + Israel's obedient response. The 'commands I have given you' regarding the Canaanite nations (the herem, complete destruction: Deuteronomy 7:1-5; Deuteronomy 20:16-18) are the referent — the conquest is not military opportunism but covenant action with specific terms of engagement.
6: 'Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid or terrified by them — the LORD your God goes with you' — the courage formula appears seven times in Joshua 1 (Joshua 1:6,7,9,18) as Joshua's commissioning refrain. Its origin here, on Moses's lips to the entire assembled people, establishes it as the foundational courage-theology of the conquest. The theological basis is not military confidence or numerical advantage but the divine presence: 'the LORD your God goes with you.' The 'he will never leave you or abandon you' (lōʾ yarpĕkā wĕlōʾ yaʿazbĕkkā) echoes the covenant-loyalty formula and becomes the direct content of YHWH's own assurance to Joshua in Deuteronomy 31:8. Hebrews 13:5 quotes this promise as grounding Christian contentment: 'I will never leave you nor forsake you.'
7: 'Moses summoned Joshua and said to him before all Israel, Be strong and courageous' — the public commissioning of Joshua is a formal ceremony of leadership transition. 'Before all Israel' is the witness-community of the covenant assembly; the commissioning must be public to establish Joshua's legitimate authority over the entire people. The formula 'you will be the one who takes them in to possess it' (ʾattāh tābōʾ ʾet-hāʾāreṣ) is the fulfillment language — Joshua's role is defined by the promise to the ancestors that has not yet been realized. He is the executor of the ancestral covenant's land-clause.
8: 'The LORD himself goes before you. He will be with you; he will never leave you or abandon you' — this verse repeats Deuteronomy 31:6's assurance but now applies it specifically to Joshua, not to the whole people. The shift from 'us' (Deuteronomy 31:6: 'Do not be afraid or terrified') to direct address of Joshua makes the assurance personal and covenantal. Joshua is receiving what Moses received at the burning bush: the presence-promise that grounds leadership. The 'do not be afraid or discouraged' (lōʾ tîrāʾ wĕlōʾ tēḥāt) covers both external danger and internal psychological wavering — the completeness of the divine support leaves no category of fear unaddressed.
9: 'Moses wrote down this law and handed it over to the Levitical priests who carried the ark of the covenant' — the writing of the Torah is a formal act of covenant documentation. In ANE suzerainty treaties, the written treaty document was deposited in the sanctuary of both parties and was to be read publicly at regular intervals. Deuteronomy follows this pattern precisely: the Torah is written (this verse), deposited beside the ark (Deuteronomy 31:26), and commanded to be read every seven years (Deuteronomy 31:10-11). The Levitical priests (ark-carriers) and elders together receive the document, establishing dual custodianship — priestly (ritual) and civic (elders) responsibility for the covenant text.
10: 'At the end of every seven years, during the Festival of Booths in the year of debt cancellation' — the seven-year Torah reading is coordinated with the Shemitah (sabbatical year) and the Festival of Sukkot (Booths). The debt-cancellation year (shemitah, Deuteronomy 15) reset economic inequality; the Sukkot reading reset covenant knowledge. Sukkot was the most attended festival in the Jewish calendar — all pilgrims gathered in Jerusalem for eight days. The sabbatical-year timing ensured a maximum seven-year gap between public covenant-readings, with the next reading immediately after the economic reset. The combination of economic and covenantal renewal in the same year is not coincidental.
11: 'When all Israel comes to appear before the LORD your God at the place he chooses, you must read this law aloud before all Israel in their hearing' — the public Torah reading (Hebrew: haqhēl ceremony, from the root for assembly/congregation) was a mass covenant-renewal ceremony. The second-century BCE practice of public Torah reading in synagogues and the annual lectionary cycle ultimately derive from this institution. Nehemiah 8 depicts the post-exilic restoration of the Shemitah-year reading under Ezra — a deliberate reconnection with this specific Deuteronomic ordinance after the long exile-suspension.
12: 'Gather the entire people — men, women, children, and the foreigners living in your towns' — the comprehensiveness of the assembly (men, women, children, foreigners) is theologically significant. The covenant reading is not a men-only legal gathering; it is a total-community hearing. Children who cannot yet comprehend the legal content and foreigners who are not ethnic Israelites are nonetheless present — the covenant knowledge is to be shared across every demographic boundary within the town. 'So they can hear, learn to fear the LORD, and carefully follow all the words' — hearing → fear → obedience is the covenant pedagogy sequence that Deuteronomy consistently models.
13: 'The children who do not yet know the law must hear it and learn to fear the LORD your God throughout all the time you live in the land' — the generational transmission of covenant knowledge is explicitly the purpose of the children's presence. They cannot yet fully understand, but they hear; understanding will come as they mature. This reflects a model of religious formation through immersive participation before cognitive comprehension — the child's presence at the assembly creates a formative experience that precedes doctrinal understanding. The Passover Seder's pedagogical structure (the child asking 'Why do we do this?': Exodus 12:26-27) reflects the same principle.
14: 'The LORD said to Moses, The time of your death is drawing near. Call Joshua and come to the tent of meeting so I can commission him' — the divine summons to the tent of meeting is the final formal audience of Moses's life. The tent of meeting (ʾōhel môʿēd) was the sanctuary for direct divine-human encounter (Exodus 33:7-11: YHWH would speak with Moses 'face to face, as a man speaks with his friend'). This final meeting commissions Moses's successor in the same sacred space where the covenant was mediated throughout the wilderness period. The parallel to Joshua 1, where YHWH directly addresses Joshua at the Jordan crossing, completes the transition of prophetic authority.
15: 'The LORD appeared at the tent in a pillar of cloud, which stood at the entrance of the tent' — the pillar of cloud is the visible form of the divine presence that has accompanied Israel since the Exodus (Exodus 13:21-22; Deuteronomy 14:19,24; Deuteronomy 40:34-38). Its appearance at the tent entrance for Joshua's commissioning is the last appearance of the cloud-pillar in the Torah; it will not be mentioned again until the cloud fills Solomon's temple (1 Kings 8:10-11). The cloud standing at the entrance (rather than descending or entering the tent) may mark a threshold moment — YHWH is present but the era of direct tent-meeting is closing as the conquest era begins.
16: 'After that, this people will rise up and chase after the foreign gods of the land they are entering' — the divine prophecy of Israel's future apostasy is remarkable: YHWH knows and states in advance that the conquest will be followed by Canaanite-cult syncretism. This is not a conditional warning ('if you disobey') but a prophetic certainty ('you will'). The covenantal rationale for giving this prophecy is that it becomes the content of the song-witness (Deuteronomy 31:19-22) — a text that will testify against Israel when the predicted apostasy occurs. The foreknowledge does not excuse the sin; it ensures that the sin cannot later be reframed as an understandable mistake. The Judges cycle of apostasy-judgment-rescue-apostasy is the historical outworking of this prediction.
17: 'My anger will blaze against them that day, and I will abandon them and hide my face from them' — the hiding of the divine face (wĕhissartî pānay mēhem) is the most severe expression of covenantal withdrawal. Throughout the psalms, God-forsakenness is the extremity of suffering (Psalms 22:1: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'). The hiddenness is not permanent (Deuteronomy 31:18 qualifies it as an act of judgment for specific sin) but it creates the condition of total vulnerability: without divine protection, 'many disasters and troubles will overwhelm them.' Israel asking 'Have all these evils come upon us because our God is no longer with us?' is the correct diagnosis of their situation — they will understand the mechanism even as they experience the consequence.
18: 'I will certainly conceal my face that day because of all the evil they have done — they have turned to other gods' — the double certainty ('certainly conceal': wĕʾānōkî hastēr ʾastîr, infinitive absolute + finite verb) mirrors the double certainty of the covenant curses. The construction is the same as used for Israel's expected exile: emphatic certainty, not conditionality. The divine hiddenness is directly proportional to Israel's act of turning (pānāh, they have turned their face) to other gods — the divine face-hiding mirrors the human face-turning. The theological symmetry is stark: you turn away from me; I turn away from you.
19: 'Write down this song and teach it to the Israelites. Put it in their mouths, so that this song will serve as a witness' — the Song of Moses (chapter 32) is a covenant-witness document in the tradition of ANE treaty documents. Written covenant texts in the ANE served as legal witnesses: they recorded the terms and would 'testify' against a party who violated them. The Song is internalized differently from the written Torah: rather than being written and deposited (as the Torah text: Deuteronomy 31:26), it is placed in the mouths of the people — it becomes oral tradition, embedded in communal memory, harder to lose than a written scroll. This is the covenant-witness that cannot be destroyed even if the text is lost.
20: 'When I bring them into the land of milk and honey, and they eat until they are full and become prosperous, they will turn to other gods' — the prosperity-apostasy prediction echoes the pattern already identified in Deuteronomy 8:10-14 (eat-full-forget-pride) and Deuteronomy 32:15 (Jeshurun grew fat and kicked). The 'land of milk and honey' formula appears throughout Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 6:3; Deuteronomy 11:9; Deuteronomy 26:9,15; Deuteronomy 27:3) as the standard description of the land's agricultural bounty. The prediction that the land's abundance itself becomes the occasion for apostasy is the irony at the heart of the covenant: the blessing designed to deepen loyalty actually weakens it when the blessing is detached from the Blesser.
21: 'When disasters and troubles overwhelm them, this song will testify against them' — the song's legal function as witness (ʿēd, legal witness) is foregrounded. In the covenant-lawsuit genre (Hebrew rîb), the plaintiff (YHWH) would bring charges against the defendant (Israel) before a court; the witnesses (heaven, earth, the song) would testify. The prophetic lawsuit literature (Isaiah 1:2-4; Jeremiah 2:4-13; Micah 6:1-8) is exactly this form: YHWH summoning the cosmic court and presenting evidence of Israel's covenant violation. The song's being in the people's mouths means the testimony is irrefutable — they have preserved their own indictment.
22: 'Moses wrote down this song that very day and taught it to the Israelites' — the immediate execution of the divine command (write → teach) compresses the transmission chain: Moses does not merely write and deposit but personally teaches it, making him the first teacher of his own indictment. The song (Deuteronomy 32) is therefore simultaneously a Torah composition of Moses (written) and an oral tradition he inaugurated (taught). The written-and-oral duality of Torah transmission — text as deposit and recitation as living practice — is established in this single verse. Deuteronomy will also later be called both 'this book' and 'these words' to reflect the same dual mode.
23: 'The LORD then commissioned Joshua son of Nun, saying, Be strong and courageous' — Deuteronomy 31:23 distinguishes between Moses commissioning Joshua before the people (Deuteronomy 31:7) and YHWH directly commissioning Joshua at the tent. The divine direct-commission creates Joshua's prophetic authority — he is not merely Moses's designated successor but YHWH's directly appointed leader. 'I will be with you' (kî ʾānōkî ʾehyeh ʿimmāk) is the formula that YHWH used with Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:12: 'I will be with you') — the continuity of the divine-presence-with-the-leader is the thread of covenant leadership through Moses to Joshua. Joshua 1:5,9 develops this commission at length.
24: 'When Moses had finished writing out the entire law in a book' — the completion formula marks the close of the Mosaic legislative corpus. 'The entire law' (ʾet-dibrê hattôrāh-hazzōʾt) refers to Deuteronomy specifically, not the entire Pentateuch — Moses is completing the covenant document he began dictating in chapter 1. The 'book' (sēper) is a scroll; the same word is used for the book of the covenant in Exodus 24:7 (Moses reads the covenant to Israel and the people accept it). The writing and the reading are both legally significant acts — the covenant text's authority is established by its written form.
25: 'Moses gave a command to the Levites who carried the ark of the covenant of the LORD' — the depositional command goes to the ark-carrying Levites rather than to the Aaronide priests. In Deuteronomy 10:8, 'the tribe of Levi' was designated to carry the ark; in Deuteronomy 31:9, the same Levitical priests received the written Torah from Moses. The ark-and-Torah together formed the covenant archive: the tablets of the Ten Commandments inside the ark (Deuteronomy 10:5) and the book of Deuteronomy beside it (Deuteronomy 31:26). The two-layer archive reflects the two-layer covenant structure: the foundational words (Ten Commandments) and the elaborated covenant law (Deuteronomy).
26: 'Take this scroll of the law and place it beside the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God. It will remain there as a witness against you' — the law placed beside the ark parallels the ANE treaty deposit. Hittite suzerainty treaties were deposited in the sanctuaries of both parties; the treaty was to be read periodically before the divine witness. Israel's single sanctuary placed both the foundational covenant (tablets inside the ark: Deuteronomy 10:2,5) and the elaborated covenant-law (Deuteronomy beside the ark) in the same sacred deposit. The 'witness against you' function means the scroll will eventually be brought out to testify in the covenant-lawsuit when Israel violates its terms — exactly what happens in 2 Kings 22:8-13 when Hilkiah finds the book of the law in the temple and Josiah tears his garments.
27: 'I know how rebellious and stubborn you are. Even today, while I am still with you, you have been defiant' — Moses's candid assessment of Israel's character is grounded in the wilderness record: even during the forty years of divine provision, Israel complained repeatedly (Exod 16-17; Num 11; 14; 16; 20; 21). The 'even today' is the most damning element — present-tense rebellion in Moses's presence, not merely future-tense predicted rebellion. The progression ('How much worse will it be after I am gone?') uses the standard qal wahomer argument form: if the lesser is true, the greater certainly is. It is the same logic the prophets use in arguing from Israel's checkered history to their predicted future failure.
28: 'Gather all the elders of your tribes and your officials so I can speak these words in their hearing and call heaven and earth to testify against them' — the assembly of elders-and-officials parallels the covenant-assembly formula throughout Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 1:13-15; Deuteronomy 5:23; Deuteronomy 27:1; Deuteronomy 29:10). The heaven-and-earth witness invocation (also at Deuteronomy 4:26; Deuteronomy 30:19; Deuteronomy 32:1) closes the legal preparation for the Song: Moses is formally constituting the covenant-court before reciting the witness-song. Prophetic lawsuits (Isaiah 1:2: 'Hear, O heavens, and listen, O earth') draw directly on this Deuteronomic witness formula.
29: 'I know that after my death you will become deeply corrupt and turn away from everything I have commanded' — Moses's death-prophecy of Israel's apostasy is the most unambiguous statement in all of Torah that the covenant will be violated. 'After my death' (ʾaḥărê môtî) sets the timeline: the leader who has kept Israel intermittently on course for forty years will be gone, and the centrifugal forces of Canaanite culture will prove too strong for the unled community. 'What your hands make' — the idol-making of Exodus 32 (the golden calf made while Moses was on the mountain) is the paradigm; the predicted apostasy is a return to the type already established. Deuteronomy's honest pessimism about Israel's covenant faithfulness is the theological backdrop for every prophetic indictment from Amos through Malachi.
30: 'Moses then recited the words of this song in the hearing of the entire assembly of Israel, from beginning to end' — the chapter closes with the public recitation that Deuteronomy 31:22 prepared. The Song of Moses (Deuteronomy 32) is not merely a written deposit but a performed act: Moses himself delivers it to the full assembly. The phrase 'from beginning to end' (lĕtumām, to their completion) ensures the complete transmission — no verse is skipped, no section summarized. The oral performance by the author before the entire community is the founding act of the Song's transmission as both oral tradition and written text, establishing a dual-mode preservation parallel to Deuteronomy 31:19's 'put it in their mouths.'
